sentences.text: Good morning.
I was born in Warsaw, Poland in 1909.
I was born to a family-- a middle-class family.
A very-- my father was a very religious man.
The house was very religious.
We were-- together, we were six children.
We were four sisters, two brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] During the-- this is in the time from the First World War.
We were very comfortable.
After the war, we-- we were not-- and during the war-- we were not comfortable enough.
The sort of not only our family, but a lot of families not comfortable.
And the question about food was very hard.
My younger sister got sick.
She died.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My father was sick, so he went to his rabbi, which he was a hosid from that rabbi, [NON-ENGLISH].
He went to him, asked him what he should do with the rest of the family?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The rabbi told him, Shloime-- that's the name of my father-- go home and take care of your wife and the rest children.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
He came back, and he said to my mother, Fiege, the rabbi told me I should move away from that place where I'm living now and move somewhere else.
My mother-- being born in a little town in Poland of the name Laskarkzew-- so she had there some family and friends.
We sold everything, you know, what my mother had to accumulate, a few zlotys then-- I mean, rubles, what they had that time, and went to that town, to Laskarkzew.
We came to that town.
We didn't have where to go, and we went into my mother's father's sister, and we stayed there a few days and then we found a home somewhere, an apartment we rented and we moved in.
During this time, my father got sick just from hunger, and he died coming to Laskarkzew-- he died in Laskarkzew.
Then my older sister, she got sick and she died.
My mother remained with four children-- two brothers, two sisters.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She tried to do her best to keep up her children.
She had the rich brothers in Lodz.
She went to them, and she asked for help.
They helped her.
She came back, and she went to another town somewhere what she knows she has some family.
This is Demblin.
She came TO-- to Demblin, and [NON-ENGLISH], in Demblin, was her-- her father's brother.
He said, look, Fiege, you don't have to work so hard.
I will find for you a husband.
He found a husband for her and she got married the second time.
Avram-- Abraham Silverman.
She married.
Where she could ot have-- he had six children, and we were four.
So we couldn't get together.
You see, it would be 10 together.
So she sent away my brother and my sister to Lodz, to her family.
And I, being a boy from seven years, eight years old at that time, and my younger sister-- she was a baby about four, fives years old, you know?
We remained with her-- with my mother.
After a few years, I got-- you know, then I-- you know, they send me to cheder, and I went to school there.
So the time was passing by until I became 10 or 12 years old and I said I cannot be together here.
Fighting between the-- his children.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I picked up myself.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I went to Warsaw.
I came to Warsaw, and I went into my mother's sister.
She was living in Warsaw on the [NON-ENGLISH].
I said, Tante, you have to give me-- I've not where to sleep.
12, 13 Years old.
[CRYING] Said, don't worry.
We going-- we are here, seven, it's going to be an eighth one.
Made me a place, and I was-- I was there.
And I start to go find a trade.
I should work.
And through the-- Demblin, the-- you know, we had some-- made friendship there with people.
They had somebody there in Warsaw living on of [NON-ENGLISH].
So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So they told me, you know, my father was a religious man, mothers, brothers were religious.
What are you going to be?
You going to be a schnabel?
You going to be a shyster?
Doesn't fit in into our family, you know?
Still, they want-- they want me to be a [NON-ENGLISH].
A [NON-ENGLISH] means you making the upper parts from the shoe.
The upper part-- doesn't do nothing with the-- with the bottom part.
You know.
Yeah, that's-- a shoemaker is [LAUGHS] to sit low and he sits high.
You know?
So-- I went in there, and I start to learn the trade, and I-- they know me, they know the family.
So I was there with food and with sleeping because I didn't have where to sleep, so I worked during that.
And so this passed by some time.
Then my brother came back from Lodz, and he was in wars.
He was older.
He was three years older than I, and my other sister was also three years older than my brother.
They had worked already in places.
All-- my brother worked also in the same trade what I did, and he helped me, and you know-- and I-- became a [NON-ENGLISH].
[LAUGHS] This-- the life was a hard life.
I was already an independent person.
I worked for myself. and I made a living for myself, a few zlotys.
And so-- you know, and then they-- you have to belong to a trade union.
Being that I belonged to the trade union, and life was going on-- not too, too good, but I lived.
I lived and I grew up.
I went to, you know, night courses in Warsaw, you know-- to learn a little bit, the writing and the reading and everything.
I went to-- I cannot live like that.
Every-- every night, for a long time, I went to school and I was working during the day.
And I-- so I kept myself.
You know, I became independent-- not to my mother, not to nobody.
You know, nobody didn't needed the help.
I was 15, 16 years old.
I was a grown-up man.
It's like-- Because I-- I felt, you know, the-- then I have to do it.
If I would not do it for myself, nobody will help me.
My mother couldn't help me, my sisters couldn't help the brothers cannot help me.
I have to help myself.
So I try to do it, whatever I could.
And I did it.
When you came to Warsaw, you said you were 12 or 13 years old.
Where did you become bar mitzvah'd?
I didn't have a bar mitzvah.
Let me tell you the truth-- I didn't have a bar mitzvah like-- like we have here, bar mitzvahs, with my grandchildren.
I knew it when I was 13 years old, and I started to put up the tefillin, you know
Mine mother's sister had a son-- a son.
He was a very religious man.
Very religious man.
And he said, look, Simon, [NON-ENGLISH], you have to have tefillin.
I said I haven't got money to buy tefillin.
I'm going to give you money.
I'm going to give you not money-- I'm going to give you tefillin.
And you should-- if I'm going to give you the tefillin, you have to put them on every day, And how to put it on, I will teach you how to put it on.
So this is the way how I got the bar mitzvah.
I didn't got a bar mitzvah parties.
They're calling me off here in the temple with that.
Absolutely not.
I didn't know anything about that.
When you were on your own in Warsaw, were you [NON-ENGLISH]?
Shabbis?
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There was no different for me than shabbis either one.

I'm telling you the truth-- that's not-- it was no different than-- because I didn't feel, I didn't have that feeling from the shabbis to sit at the table together with the-- with the-- yeah, I had that feeling when I was a young kid, six years old, and my father was still alive and we lived in Warsaw.
We were at that time, before the war, very comfortable-- not comfortable, but very comfortable.
I remember my father from Passover night, to wear that kittle and to sit down at the table and the table was served with the best-- and with-- and you were [INAUDIBLE] for the glass of wine.
It was wonderful.
This I had in my mind.
You know, I lived with that-- with the dreams from yesterday, not from today.
Today was a very bad life.
And I started as soon as my father died, as soon as my mother married-- married the second time.
My life, it started to get harder and harder.
[CRYING] But I thank God I survived.
I survived and I lived.
I lived in Warsaw.
Then my mother lived in Demblin.
You see my mother lived with that second husband in Demblin.
Then they decided they're going to move to Otwock-- that Otwock, it was a town-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] near Warsaw.
But when I got older already, I came and I worked in Warsaw, and moving-- my mother is not-- said, look, I'm going to sleep over here, and I'm going to work in the town.
I'm going to have a ticket and go every day for the work.
So I did it.
I worked in Warsaw.
It was hard.
I couldn't make that everything, you know-- and I came every night, I had that ticket for a month, r for a week.
And every night I came home.
Well, I kept myself-- I didn't need my mother at that time, anything.
We lived-- my brother together.
My brother and sister, the older ones, they never came home.
They lived in Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And when I became-- when I got older, I came with this-- you know, this was already 18, 19 years, 20 years old.
So now that's starting army.
You know, at 21 years, I have to go to the army.
I want you to-- I want you to know one thing, that until 21, until I was 20 years, I didn't know when I was born.
My mother, according to her telling me, it is at this and this time you were born, this and this month you were born, it was [NON-ENGLISH], it was that.
Then we figured out this was the 1st of June, 1909.
We figured it out, and that's all I remained with. 1909 is my birthday.
Now at 21 years, I have to go to the army.
You know?
And they took me, that army, and was-- you know, and I became a soldier.
That was, I mean-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was but 21 months, 22 months in the army.

Life-- coming to the army, you know, even being in the army, you needed also some help, financially help, because the food what you got there was not sufficient, you see, to keep up yourself and your energy and everything.
Usually most of the soldiers, they got packages from home for food.
They got the zlotys, and they bought some food.
My mother tried-- I don't know how she tried, what she did, what.
She tried to send me all this-- the zlotys, I should be able to buy some food.
And I lived.
Matter of fact, I have here a picture which I came to my brother.
He was-- he was married.
That's the story.
He got married with a cousin, my mother's brother's daughter, and he lived in Lodz.
And he want me always to come.
I should come to him.
We're going to be together.
I said-- I don't want to be with nobody together.
You see, I want to be by myself.
And-- you know, when I had the free-- some holidays, I got the, you know, a--
Pass to go to wherever I want to, so I went to Lodz.
I went there.
But in Lodz-- as a matter of fact, I have a picture here when I was a soldier, and walking with my brother, with my-- he got married, and he had a little boy.
You know, and I was-- I finish my army at 21.
Anti-Semitism was all over in Poland.
That's not just the army.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] You know, we lived through times in Poland till the army.
There are certain places you were afraid to go-- afraid to go out at night.
You know, they-- they-- Jew and Jew and-- you know.
It was not too comfortable, even in the army when I was there.
Or, it was a little bit, you know, more curbed with the anti-Semitism.
They didn't show it so much.
You know, you-- you could not advance to being an officer.
They have too-- too little, too single ones to be an officer on the Polish army.
But I was there, but between the soldiers I didn't feel.
You see-- the-- because I did that what-- it belong to a soldier to do, to march and to go and to the-- [NON-ENGLISH] and everything.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
I worked at my trade which-- got the job, and I worked on my trade.
And I was traveling every day.
I worked in Warsaw and in [NON-ENGLISH].
And, you know, this was, I would say, was already in the '20s, you know, the late '20s.
No, the beginning of the '30s.
Beginning of the '30s, the middle '30s.
And I belong to the trade unions.
And I worked with them together.
I was already a completely grown up person coming out of the army.
A completely grown up person.
I took care of myself.
And I always cared about my mother.
The whole family was very close to each other.
The sister was in Warsaw, brother in Warsaw, and the younger sister was married.
I had a younger sister, she got married.
And then, I-- you know, being in Polska, I went out with some girls, and I found a girl, which I married her.
Her name was Frania.
Fra-- yeah.
But this was close to the war.
I lived in Otwock at that time.
Then the war broke out.
I was there in Otwock.

That start-- we run away, we knew then we can-- you know, the Jews, they cannot be in Poland because the Germans will-- they were very-- we heard from some other places, you know, and from Germany, the crystal night.
We knew everything.
Hitler's speeches we heard on the radio.
So everybody start to run, and run away, and run away, run to the east, more the east, more to Russia side.
I was among this people which there was running, and I was also there.
And I came to Bialystok, and I was in [NON-ENGLISH], Litovsk.
And coming there, I found out my brother is there, and mine sister, mine older sister is there.
So I have to contact them to be together.
We should be together.
My brother said, look, when I found them in Bialystok, he looked at me and said, Simon, I have to do something.
I have to-- he was married with a child.
I have to bring the maman, and I have to bring my wife and the child.
I said, look, I was considered at that time more stronger than he.
You know, I was-- I was more healthier.
And I said, don't worry.
I'm going to try to go back to Poland, you see, and bring the family.
And then we're going to be-- then we're going to see what we have to do it.
And I picked up myself, another few people, and we start to go back.
This was in wintertime, we start to go back to Poland, to the occupied territories from the Germans.
We were in the occupied territory at that time with the Russian.
From the Russian side we went to the German, and when we came there, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was impossible to go back again.
So I remained stuck there in Poland and the-- with the Germans.
I went-- yeah, I went back to Otwock.
And then I found out that my brother, where my brother is there.
And I couldn't do anything to take my mother and my sister-in-law and the baby-- and go back to the other side, to the Russian side.
It was impossible already.
All the borders were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] guard very, very good.
And I remain in that-- in Otwock.
